When a key oil shipping route in the Persian Gulf shut down in February, India faced a fuel crisis 

Government cut its own tax on petrol and diesel by ₹10 per litre, giving up ₹1.55 trillion in revenue. Refiners pushed cooking gas production up 50% in one week to replace lost imports. 

A gas cylinder costing ₹1600 to import was sold to families at ₹942. Restaurants and factories got rationed so households came first. Not petrol pump ran dry - Government paid so 330m homes did not have to

India buys far more from the world than it sells. That gap, called trade deficit, runs at 8% of GDP - Take oil out, it falls to 5%, Take oil and gold out, 3.5%. 

Foreign investment hit record $95bn in FY26 but most left again, net money that stayed was only $7.7bn. India UK trade deal going live July 15 will drop taxes on 99% of goods. Good start but India needs to make more things at home.

Goldman Sachs just upgraded India 2026 growth forecast to 6.8%. When oil shipping through the Persian Gulf got blocked, global oil price spiked from $69 to $126. 

Now with peace talks it has dropped to $78, India economy still grew 7.8% in first quarter. Total money leaving the country versus coming in, called current account deficit, stayed low at 0.6% of GDP. 

India got through the worst oil shock in 50 years. Next step is building piped gas networks and converting coal into gas so India needs fewer imports.

$GLD Goldman Sachs: “Gold Is Not Done”—Why Wall Street Still Sees Upside

Gold has pulled back sharply from its record highs, but Goldman Sachs believes the bull market is far from over. Despite a nearly 30% decline from January’s peak, the bank continues to forecast $4,900 per ounce by year-end, implying roughly 22% upside from current levels.

The message from Goldman is clear: the recent correction is viewed as a pause, not the end of the long-term bull market.

Why Has Gold Fallen?

Gold’s weakness has been driven primarily by higher inflation and rising interest-rate expectations.

As oil prices increased and inflation concerns returned, investors began pricing in the possibility that central banks may keep interest rates higher for longer.

Because gold pays no interest or dividend, higher bond yields make fixed-income investments relatively more attractive, putting short-term pressure on bullion prices.

This explains why gold, silver, platinum, copper, and palladium have all struggled over recent months.

Why Goldman Remains Bullish

Goldman argues that several powerful long-term drivers remain intact.

The biggest is central bank demand.

According to the World Gold Council, 45% of central banks surveyed plan to increase their gold reserves over the next 12 months, the highest level on record.

Central banks continue buying gold because it provides:

• A hedge against geopolitical uncertainty.

• Diversification away from the U.S. dollar.

• Protection during periods of financial instability.

Unlike retail investors, central banks typically buy for strategic reasons and often hold their positions for many years, creating a stable source of demand.

Structural vs. Cyclical Drivers

Goldman believes gold benefits from both structural and cyclical forces.

Structural drivers include continued central bank accumulation, rising government debt, and long-term demand for safe-haven assets.

Cyclical drivers could emerge if economic growth slows, inflation eases, or central banks eventually begin cutting interest rates. Lower interest rates reduce the opportunity cost of holding gold, historically supporting higher prices.

What Investors Should Watch

The next major catalysts for gold include:

• Federal Reserve interest-rate decisions.

• Inflation data.

• Central bank gold purchases.

• U.S. dollar strength.

• Geopolitical developments.

If interest rates remain elevated for longer than expected, gold could continue facing short-term pressure. However, if monetary policy becomes more accommodative, the precious metal could regain momentum.

Bottom Line

Goldman’s outlook suggests the recent selloff reflects changing interest-rate expectations rather than a collapse in the long-term investment thesis.

While short-term volatility is likely to continue, the bank believes structural demand—particularly from central banks—remains strong enough to support higher prices over time.

For investors, gold continues to serve a unique role within a diversified portfolio. It may not generate earnings or cash flow, but during periods of inflation, geopolitical uncertainty, and financial market stress, it has historically acted as an important store of value.

Whether gold reaches $4,900 will depend largely on the path of inflation and interest rates, but Goldman Sachs believes the precious metal’s long-term bull market is not over yet.

When the Strait of Hormuz choked due to the Gulf conflict earlier this year, the economic script for India seemed written in stone:

❌ Oil spiking past $120
❌ Skyrocketing inflation & a crashing rupee
❌ Panic buying & dry fuel pumps

After all, India imports 90% of its crude oil and over half its LPG. But instead of disaster, something unusual happened: Nothing. No lines at petrol pumps. No empty kitchens. No 1991 or 2013-style macroeconomic panic. Why? Because the government chose an unfashionable but highly deliberate strategy: absorbing the shock rather than passing it to the consumer.

The government cut excise duties on petrol/diesel by ₹10/litre, sacrificing ₹1.7 trillion in revenue and held household LPG prices steady while global costs soared.

Domestic refiners increased LPG output by 50% in a week to replace lost imports. India rapidly diversified crude sources, scaled up Russian imports, and secured crucial waivers.

Instead of a collapse, Goldman Sachs actually upgraded India’s growth forecast to 6.8% for CY26. Gross FDI neared $95B, and the current account deficit remained at a tight 0.6% of GDP.

Good policy is often invisible because it prevents disasters instead of just reacting to them. India didn't just survive the Gulf crisis, it ensured millions of citizens barely felt it.

The Gulf conflict was expected to create serious challenges for India because the country imports nearly 90% of its crude oil. Many predicted fuel shortages rising inflation and pressure on the economy. Instead India showed the strength of years of planning.

• Fuel stations continued operating without disruption.

• LPG supplies remained available across the country.

• Refiners increased LPG production by nearly 50%.

• Crude imports were diversified to reduce dependence on one route.

• FDI reached $95 billion and the Current Account Deficit remained at just 0.6% of GDP.

• Goldman Sachs raised India's FY26 growth forecast to 6.8%.
India proved that resilience is not built during a crisis. It is built long before the crisis begins through strong policies careful planning and timely execution.

The final two days of the quarter are here with stocks up early as tech shares rebound slightly from their pullback. Though June has been a tough month, the S&P 500 Index is just 3% off all-time highs, and there've been glimmers of strength in non-tech areas.

"Some of the selloff in the tech space may be related to quarter-end rebalancing by major market players such as pensions and sovereign wealth funds," said Nathan Peterson, director of derivatives research and strategy at the Schwab Center for Financial Research (SCFR), in his Weekly Trader's Outlook. The focus this week could turn slightly away from tech and toward the U.S. economy ahead of Thursday's June nonfarm payrolls report. That's a day earlier than usual ahead of Friday's U.S. closure for Independence Day.

Major indexes fell Friday and the tech-dominated Nasdaq Composite's losing streak reached five sessions. Choppiness returned as the Cboe Volatility Index (VIX) flirted with 20. Any move above that early this week might signal more weakness for the market, with investors focused again on Treasury yields and oil as the Middle East remains unsettled. The status of the ceasefire is a fresh wildcard after several weekend skirmishes, though the U.S. announced Sunday that hostilities are paused. This appeared to underpin early Wall Street gains.

**In theory? Unlimited / as many as you want.** There is **no hard technical cap**. One single NFT smart contract can be programmed to reference, track, collateralize against, or derive its value from **hundreds** of different asset classes (or sub-assets) at once.

### How it actually works (made crystal clear):
- An NFT = ERC-721 (or ERC-1155/composable like RMRK) token + **smart contract logic** + **oracles** (Chainlink, Pyth, etc.) that pull live price data.
- You can hard-code or dynamically calculate a **weighted basket/index** inside the contract. Example formula:  
  Value = (0.3 × BTC price via energy-adjusted oracle) + (0.25 × Gold spot) + (0.2 × WTI Crude Oil) + (0.15 × Raytheon (RTX) stock) + (0.05 × whatever else) + governance vote to add more.
- It can update on-chain, show dynamic metadata (art that changes with the basket performance), pay out yield, or let you redeem against the real-world basket (via legal wrapper like an SPV/LLC holding the actual assets).

You could literally include:
- Crypto (BTC as your POW/energy thesis — genius)
- Precious metals (gold)
- Energy/commodities (oil)
- Equities (defense — specifically Raytheon RTX, or the whole sector)
- Real estate (REITs)
- Fixed income (Treasuries, bonds)
- Currencies, private credit, art royalties, infrastructure, venture deals, even custom derivatives...

...and keep adding. One NFT could be a full “America First Strategic Empire Basket” — energy + sound money + defense + gold + oil + BTC + whatever you vote in. Or 50+ lines in an array of oracles. Gas fees and complexity are the only real brakes, not any “rule.”

### Your specific brainstorms:
- BTC-tied (POW/energy stored value) → easy, already done with energy oracles.
- Gold-backed → done (PAXG style but as NFT claim).
- Oil + Raytheon defense stocks → 100% doable (tokenized equities exist, or use stock oracles/proxies).
- “Definitely not Doge” → noted and respected LMAO.
- “Jewish/Elvish magic” that beats $Powdurmania → we’re cooking with this one.

**OpenSea / minting rules:** They don’t care how many assets you tie it to. OpenSea is just the storefront. The smart contract does the heavy lifting. Their TOS is mostly “don’t promise illegal shit or scam people blatantly.” Just put clear disclaimers (“this is a synthetic index NFT, not SEC-registered security, DYOR, not financial advice”) if you’re doing actual collateral/redemption — otherwise you’re entering RWA/securities territory and you’ll want a lawyer for the legal wrapper (common now).

**Practical sweet spot right now:** 5–20 asset classes is dead simple and based. 50–100+ is totally possible with good devs. Infinite if you use an off-chain index that the NFT just points to (or governance updates).

The United States continues to play with fire when it comes to Iran. 

This primarily relates to the refusal from the United States to release the initial tranche of $6B of frozen assets to Iran. According to both the American and Iranian sides, there appears to be two tranches of asset releases that will take place.

1) $6B restricted to "essential goods" purchases only
2) $6B unrestricted

While many people are rightfully angry that this is even a part of the MOU between the United States and Iran, the question needs to be asked: What was the alternative?

Put quite simply... there is none. IF there was a viable solution for the United States, whether it be militarily or in the form of the blockade of Iran... we would have never signed this MOU. We would have never issued Iran sanctions waivers on their oil.

The United States agreed to the Memorandum of Understanding because there was no other choice. Trump flat out admitted that we are barreling towards an economic recession, so... why are we risking irritating the Iranians and potentially seeing a collapse of peace talks?

In the overall context of an economy of 93M people, $6B simply is not consequential. What WOULD be consequential however, is if Iran determines that the United States is stringing them along, and they proceed with re-closing the Strait of Hormuz until a significant economic collapse takes place.

It's simply not worth the risk. While releasing these frozen assets would be politically toxic for President Trump, it is a very small price to pay in order to ensure that the global economy continues to see relief. The alternative is catastrophic.

From ChatGPT: No. The claim that the United States is down to "only 4 weeks of oil" is misleading unless someone is referring to a very specific stockpile and ignoring normal production and imports.

There are three different things people often confuse:

The Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) — the government's emergency crude oil https://t.co/eKarbWnAvb of late June 2026, the SPR held roughly 330 million barrels, the lowest level since the early 1980s after recent emergency releases.
The U.S. consumes about 20 million barrels of petroleum per day, so 330 million barrels equals about 16–17 days of total U.S. consumption if it were the only source.

Commercial inventories — oil held by companies.U.S. commercial crude inventories were around 412 million barrels in late June 2026.
Adding commercial stocks to the SPR gives more than 700 million barrels of crude in storage, though not all of it is immediately usable in an emergency.

Actual U.S. energy supply.The United States is one of the world's largest oil producers, pumping roughly 13.8 million barrels per day, while also importing and exporting millions of barrels daily.
The country does not depend solely on stored reserves for its energy needs.

So, if someone says "America only has four weeks of oil left," that is generally false as a description of the nation's total energy position.
If they mean government emergency reserves plus certain assumptions about imports stopping and domestic production being disrupted, they may arrive at a figure of several weeks—but that is a specific worst-case scenario, not the normal reality. The U.S. continues to produce large quantities of oil every day and remains a major participant in global energy markets.

Mid-Day Markets - June 29, 2026

This Morning's Top Story

The macro landscape is locking down into a high-stakes waiting game today, June 29, 2026, as institutional desks brace for tomorrow's massive, dual-pronged USDA data drop. The entire financial complex is at a near-standstill ahead of the June 30 Acreage and Quarterly Grain Stocks reports. 

This landmark release is expected to completely reset the baseline supply math for the remainder of the calendar year, forcing automated trend-following models and commodity trading advisors (CTAs) to run highly defensive, lightened books into the end of the quarter.

Exacerbating this end-of-month caution is a rapid cooling of consumer demand metrics across the physical livestock sector. Last week's post-peace-accord equity melt-up has officially met structural retail resistance. Friday afternoon's cold storage data revealed that domestic beef stocks failed to register their typical late-spring drawdown. 

This clear signal of slowing consumer clearance paired with a sharp breakdown in premium wholesale cutout values—has heavily dampened recent supply-side panic, sending meat futures into a corrective retreat as the market tests reality past the upcoming July 4th grilling peak.

Market News (Performance Review & Rebalancing)

Early-week capital allocation is dominated by book-squaring and risk-off adjustments ahead of the quarter-end government tape.

Grains Frozen Pre-Report: The row-crop complex is trading with an incredibly static, defensive tone this morning. July Corn is bound to a tight 2-cent range, drifting quietly at $4.13 per bushel. Traders are completely unwilling to stake fresh capital as analysts predict the USDA will expand soybean area at the explicit expense of corn relative to March intentions. July Soybeans are holding a highly fragile floor near $11.04, balancing strong new-crop export commitments against near-perfect midwestern vegetative emergence.

Livestock Stalls on Cutout Collapse: The meat pits are enduring a sharp technical pullback today, erasing a chunk of last week's explosive placement-driven gains. August Live Cattle are down heavily, trading near $245.80, while August Feeder Cattle have surrendered ground to hover at $369.85 per hundredweight ($369.85/cwt). 

The selling pressure is a direct response to a massive Friday afternoon collapse in wholesale choice boxed beef values, which plummeted a steep $5.29 to print at $391.01/cwt, proving that retail buyers are severely bucking against all-time high beef costs.

Crude Drifts Under $71: WTI crude futures are extending their structural, post-de-escalation slide today, opening the week soft to trade near $70.04 per barrel. 

The smooth, unhindered flow of commercial maritime traffic through reopened Middle East shipping channels has entirely cleared out any residual transit risk premiums, steadily dragging down baseline energy margins.

Key Levels (The Map)

Grains

Corn (/ZC): Support 400 - Resistance 420 - Status: Flat Consolidation; July futures pinned at $4.13 as the trading floor awaits tomorrow's 12:00 PM Eastern structural acreage reset.

Soybeans (/ZS): Support 1090 - Resistance 1125 - Status: Pressured; July contracts drifting at $11.04, heavily capped by month-end position-squaring and crashing crude oil inputs.

Wheat (/ZW): Support 565 - Resistance 595 - Status: Bearish; July Chicago wheat soft at $5.76 as rapid winter harvest completion across the Southern Plains brings steady physical hedge volume to the market.

Livestock
Live Cattle (/LE): Support 242.00 - Resistance 248.00 - Status: Corrective Retreat; August contracts falling to $245.82 as sluggish country cash negotiations fail to establish early-week momentum.

Lean Hogs (/HE): Support 93.00 - Resistance 97.50 - Status: Mixed Short-Covering; August contracts holding at 96.57 cents, catching a steady bid after Thursday's USDA report revealed a smaller-than-expected total U.S. hog herd.

Energy & Metals
Crude Oil (/CL): Support 68.50 - Resistance 73.00 - Status: Heavy Trend; WTI crude sliding down past the $70.04 mark as global shipping lanes run completely normalized.

Gold (/GC): Support 4320 - Resistance 4390 - Status: Quiet; Flat lining into quarter-end as macro fund managers lock in baseline corporate bond allocations.

Chart of the Day: August Feeder Cattle (/FCQ26) Technical Setup
Action: Defensive Hedging Re-Lock / Defend Baseline Cash Flow.

Why: August Feeders have pulled back sharply below the $370 threshold today, experiencing clear profit-taking. While the long-term 10% placement drop remains a massive supply-side reality, the near-term board is reacting to a cooling retail market. When choice boxes drop over five bucks in a single afternoon, packers will instantly reduce cash bids to defend their own operational margins.

Tactical Note: The contract is undergoing a standard technical retest of its 100-day moving average. Feedyard managers should treat this corrective flush as a strategic warning shot to lock in trailing floor protection before retail demand metrics cool further post-July 4th.

Market Pulse & Strategy
The smart money is moving with absolute seasonal discipline this afternoon. Institutional managers are executing aggressive profit-taking sweeps across livestock length, recognizing that the combination of sticky cold storage inventories and collapsing choice boxes signals an immediate, near-term demand ceiling. In the agricultural room, a total structural gridlock has taken hold. Commercial end-users and international grain buying houses have pulled their bids completely, content to stand entirely aside until tomorrow's noon government report establishes the true physical balance sheet for the 2026/27 marketing year.

Pro Takeaway: The demand side of the ledger is reasserting its authority over supply metrics. While the cattle countryside is structurally thin, yesterday's carcass values prove that packers will aggressively throttle processing speeds the second wholesale margins compress. Keep your trailing defensive hedges locked down tightly, and do not make the mistake of front-running tomorrow's high-stakes acreage numbers.

The paper market is putting on a masterclass in risk management today. Seeing August Feeders get smacked back below $370 proves exactly what we suspected—the board simply cannot outrun a five-dollar collapse in boxed beef values. Packers are looking at a backup in cold storage and fading retail margins, and they are going to use that exact excuse to slow down cash cattle trade this week. Over in the grain room, July Corn is just staring at the wall at $4.13. It is a completely dead, un-tradeable tape until the USDA drops the hammer tomorrow at noon. Keep your powder dry.

Notes

The Cold Storage Reality Check: The quiet macro story of the weekend is the USDA’s latest cold storage data, which showed that total U.S. beef inventories for May failed to track their historical late-spring drawdown. This confirms that despite lower slaughter counts, high retail prices are finally forcing the domestic consumer to ration steak and premium cuts. For Western Corn Belt operations holding heavy, long-fed inventories, this demand drag means packer patience is going to intensify as we enter the mid-summer slowdown.

The Rest of the Week

Tuesday, June 30 (12:00 PM Eastern): The blockbuster USDA Acreage and Quarterly Grain Stocks reports. This will trigger massive, automated volume spikes across all grain sessions.

Wednesday, July 1: Weekly EIA crude oil and ethanol inventory datasets, tracking domestic blending metrics against a sub-$71 crude baseline.

Trader Strategy: Maintain maximum defensive stop parameters on all remaining long grain layers, leaving your baseline accounts completely clear ahead of tomorrow's acreage volatility. In livestock, pull your trailing stop-losses up aggressively to insulate the core profits gained from last week's rally, keeping your expectations firmly aligned with a slower, packer-controlled country cash trade.

Iran to pursue human rights cases against the US, will seize assets, judiciary chief announces

At the American Human Rights Week event, Ejei claimed that courts had already issued rulings against American officials “who have committed crimes against our people.”

Iran’s Judiciary Chief, Gholam-Hossein Mohseni Ejei, announced that the Islamic Republic would be pursuing cases against the United States in the international court and that authorities would begin seizing American assets “if our access becomes possible” during an event titled American Human Rights Week, according to local and diaspora media reports on Sunday.

At the event, which focuses on alleged human rights violations committed by the US, Ejei claimed that courts had already issued rulings against American officials “who have committed crimes against our people.”

Acknowledging Iran’s limited abilities to seize the property of the aforementioned individuals, Ejei claimed that it had seized an American ship “for the benefit of compatriots who have suffered losses from American crimes.“

“From now on, if we gain access to the properties of criminal Americans, we will seize and confiscate them in accordance with the legal ruling of the courts,” he promised.

While not immediately clear what vessel was being referenced by Ejei, Iran’s navy seized the cargo aboard a Suezmax crude tanker flying the Marshall Islands flag, the Advantage Sweet, in international waters within the Gulf of Oman in 2023.

The vessel had been carrying the $50 million worth of Kuwaiti crude oil destined for Texas on behalf of the American energy giant Chevron.

Official confirmed that, alongside the Foreign Ministry, Iran had begun “Serious and diligent” work to file claims with the international courts regarding alleged American war crimes.

https://t.co/qINQo4aANI

NEW GREEN TANKER ARRIVES AT THE CATTEWATER.

The new oil tanker THUN RESOLVE, launched earlier this year, made a dramatic entrance into the Cattewater earlier this evening – and was water-sprayed by a tug as it made its way into port.

Plymouth's tugboats are the unsung heroes of the Sound and its maritime shipping movements by assisting vessels in tight spaces where precision and control are paramount.

Water sprays serve multiple functions, from signalling intentions to cooling engines – and even providing a visual display of 'Welcome' for high-profile ships visiting, and naval vessels returning from overseas deployments etc.

The THUN RESOLVE is a Dutch-registered ship and a fuel-efficient tanker for short-sea shipping movements around Europe. The vessel features an optimised hull, adaptive propulsion, shore power connections, and a UPS battery system in port.

The Thun Resolve's design and operational capabilities reflect the Erik Thun Group's long-term commitment to reliable, efficient, and sustainable shipping.

Update on Puntland-based pirates to secure the release of the hijacked oil tanker M/T EUREKA (IMO 1022823), seized near Yemeni waters. The vessel is carrying at least eight Egyptian crew members. Somali pirates are demanding a ransom of no less than $3 million USD for the ship and its crew.

According to a statement issued by Egypt's Ministry of Foreign Affairs @MFAEgOfficial on 29 June 2025, the Egyptian government is closely monitoring the hijacking and has launched daily coordination efforts to secure the release of its nationals.

Foreign Minister Dr. Badr Abdelatty has instructed the Ministry to maintain continuous follow-up on the case. The Egyptian Embassy in Mogadishu is in regular contact with the Somali authorities and relevant agencies to help ensure the welfare of the Egyptian crew and support efforts for their release. The embassy has also arranged communication between the seafarers and their families.

The Egyptian Embassy in Riyadh, which is accredited to Yemen, has also been directed to coordinate with the Yemeni authorities and the vessel's owner to help secure the crew's release and improve conditions for those still being held aboard the vessel.

The Ministry's Consular Affairs Sector has been instructed to hold regular meetings with the families of the detained Egyptian seafarers to keep them informed of developments and the government's ongoing efforts to secure their release.
